metadata.title: Program Launch & Game Readiness Review
core_topics: Studio mindset and professional launch standards
AI responsibility and ethical AI use
ai_focus: AI responsibility and ethical use
AI literacy
Human judgment in AI decision-making
Verifying and checking AI outputs before acting on them
Responsible AI use in game development and goal-setting
AI in testing and planning workflows
Risks of over-reliance on AI
AI-generated content review and quality standards
OECD AI competencies (K3.3, K4.1)
learning_objectives: Assess your game objectively by identifying strengths, gaps, and areas needing improvement before launch.
Define specific objectives that guide your iteration and professional growth throughout the term.
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a mandatory AI Literacy Diagnostic Test (15–20 minutes) completed via QR code or link under strict exam conditions — no AI tools or web search allowed. Students are told this is not graded but is required, and results will be shared by the teacher.

The Starter activity (Studio Mindset) is a whole-class discussion exploring what happens when games launch too early, why professional studios delay releases, and who is responsible for quality at launch. Students connect this to their own games by discussing specific things they would fix before releasing.

In Explore: Game State Review, students take a comprehensive look at their current game build across three dimensions: (1) Core Mechanics — whether main mechanics work reliably, controls respond properly, and gameplay loops function without breaking; (2) Design & Dynamics/Player Experience — whether the game is engaging, difficulty is balanced, and short sessions are enjoyable; (3) Narrative — whether the player knows what to do next and goals, rules, and progression are clear.

Portfolio Entry 17 requires students to complete a SWOT analysis of their current game using bullet points: Strengths (what works well), Weaknesses (what needs fixing), Opportunities (new ideas to improve), and Threats (risks that could block success). Students add brief explanations for each point. Skill boxes reference Computational Thinking, Problem Solving, and Critical Thinking.

In the Analyse phase, students set three Term 3 goals, each focusing on a different responsibility: game improvement, testing and feedback, and AI decision-making. Activity 1.1 asks students to choose 2–3 areas from a structured table (Player Experience & Polish, Testing & Feedback, AI Workflow & Decision-Making, Technical Stability/Mechanics) and write one SMART goal per area — including steps to achieve it and a deadline. Students are also directed to revisit Portfolio Entry 14B from Term 2 to check alignment.

The Stretch Activity (Studio Readiness Audit) challenges students to make a professional go/no-go launch decision based on five criteria: (1) Core Experience — is the main mechanic immediately clear to new players? (2) Stability & Functionality — does the game run without major bugs or crashes? (3) Player Perspective — have external playtests been conducted? (4) AI Involvement — document where AI tools supported development and identify content needing human review. (5) Studio Risk Assessment — identify the single biggest risk if launching today. Students make a final decision (Ready to release soon / Needs improvement before release / Not ready for release) with a written justification referencing player experience, stability/risk, and evidence from testing or review. This activity is not part of the portfolio and may be skipped if time is short.

Two AI reflection activities follow: Reflect: AI Responsibility asks students to write 2–3 sentences on their current understanding of AI responsibility, examples of responsible or irresponsible AI use, and rules people should follow — plus an AI literacy reflection using sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…' and 'I still needed to decide…'). Reflect: AI in Game Readiness prompts students to consider one way AI could support game readiness review, one decision that must remain human, and one risk of over-relying on AI — each tied to their own game.

The lesson closes with a tiered self-assessment slide (All/Most/Some criteria) and an optional Extension task: design a personal 'Student AI Use Code' — a one-page document titled 'My Responsible AI Pledge' listing 5 personal rules, 3 things never to do with AI, and 2 ways to verify AI outputs. Three formative MCQs on AI literacy (human judgment in AI use) are completed via Classkick.

teacher_notes.notes: Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions at the end of term.

Set the tone for Term 3 as a shift toward release readiness, not just building. Emphasise reflection on current game state and identifying gaps. Frame goal-setting as preparation for real players and real expectations. Link to UAE values: quality, responsibility, and innovation in digital products. Use the reflection question to focus on player trust and experience, not features.

Use this question to shift students from student mindset to studio mindset. Emphasise that readiness includes both the game and the team behind it. Encourage thinking beyond features: planning, testing, decisions, and responsibility. Reinforce that real studios are judged on how they prepare, not just ideas.

Curriculum Alignment
Analysing an existing digital product as a system aligns with UK D&T KS3 (1c – Systems & Control), AQA 3.1.3 (Systems Architecture – system components and limitations), IB Design Criterion C (analysis before creating a solution), and CSTA 3A-CS-02 (evaluating computing system behaviour and constraints).

Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Inspectors should see learners able to recall one or more criteria in their own words. This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing. Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Use this starter to introduce a professional game studio mindset. Ask students to think about what happens when games launch too early (bugs, poor reviews, player frustration) and why studios delay releases (testing, polish, player experience). Discuss who is responsible for quality at launch, highlighting that quality is a shared responsibility across a team.

Explain that this activity helps students take an honest snapshot of where their game currently stands before real players experience it. Start with Core Mechanics. Ask students to check whether the main mechanics work reliably, controls respond properly, and gameplay loops don't break. Emphasise that if the mechanics fail, the rest of the game cannot succeed. Move to Design & Dynamics (Player Experience). Prompt students to think about how the game feels to play. Is it engaging or frustrating? Is the difficulty fair and enjoyable, even in short play sessions? Finish with Narrative (Clarity & Flow). Ask students to consider whether players know what to do next. Are goals, rules, and progression clear, or does the player feel lost?

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words. Make sure exemplar entries are removed. Explain that this portfolio entry asks students to evaluate their game honestly using a SWOT analysis. Emphasise that this is about reflection and planning, not criticism. Remind students what each part means: Strengths: what already works well and should be kept. Weaknesses: areas that need fixing or improvement. Opportunities: new ideas or changes that could improve the game. Threats: risks that could affect success, such as bugs, confusion, or time limits. Highlight the skills involved: Computational Thinking (breaking the game into parts), Problem Solving (deciding what to improve), and Critical Thinking (evaluating honestly).

Ensure that students keep these points concise, in their own words. Make sure exemplar entries are removed. Explain that this portfolio entry asks students to evaluate their game honestly using a SWOT analysis. Emphasise that this is about reflection and planning, not criticism. Remind students what each part means: Strengths: what already works well and should be kept. Weaknesses: areas that need fixing or improvement. Opportunities: new ideas or changes that could improve the game. Threats: risks that could affect success, such as bugs, confusion, or time limits. Highlight the skills involved: Computational Thinking (breaking the game into parts), Problem Solving (deciding what to improve), and Critical Thinking (evaluating honestly).

Emphasise that each student will set three clear goals, with each one focusing on a different responsibility such as game improvement, testing and feedback, or AI decision-making. Use the example goals to highlight what a strong goal looks like: it is specific, measurable, and linked to player experience or responsible AI use. Reinforce that these goals will guide their actions throughout Term 3, not just this lesson.

Link back to goals they created in Term 2. See do they still align. Explain that students are now setting clear, focused goals to guide their work in Term 3. They should choose 2–3 areas from the table, or replace one with an area that better fits their game.

This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if time is short during the lesson. Frame this as a real studio go / no-go decision, not a checklist task. Emphasise justification: students must explain why they would launch or delay. Guide focus across five professional criteria: Core experience – is the main mechanic clear immediately? Player perspective – do external players understand and enjoy it? Stability – are bugs or crashes harming trust? AI involvement – where AI helped and what still needs human review. Risk assessment – identify the single biggest launch risk honestly. Reinforce that strong studios delay launches when needed to protect quality and reputation.
This activity is not part of the portfolio or assessment. Please skip if there is no time.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today's criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

This question checks students' understanding of human judgment when using AI. It reinforces that AI can inform decisions, but humans must evaluate purpose and impact. This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick. Answer key: C

Teaching note: This question targets the misconception that confidence equals correctness in AI outputs. It reinforces the need to verify AI suggestions using human judgment. This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick. Answer key: B

Teaching note: This question highlights that AI outputs often need human refinement to meet clear standards (e.g. SMART goals). It reinforces that AI can generate ideas but cannot define quality or context on its own. This is a practice question only, to be completed in Classkick. Answer key: B

metadata.title: Defining your Launch Strategy
core_topics: Responsible AI use in game development
AI literacy and evaluating AI suggestions
ai_focus: AI brainstorming for game improvement ideas using ideamap.ai
AI vs Human task allocation in game development (Activity 2.2)
Evaluating and filtering AI-generated suggestions (Activity 2.1 Step 2 — Filter)
AI literacy: knowing when to accept or reject AI suggestions
Responsible AI use: humans retain final decision-making and accountability for launch
OECD K3.2: Deciding which tasks AI can handle vs. which require human judgement
OECD K3.3: Human judgment and evaluation of AI output
OECD K4.1: AI lacks understanding of player emotions, context, and creative goals
AI prompt engineering for game analysis and improvement brainstorming
AI Literacy Reflection on AI-supported problem definition
learning_objectives: Define the Launch Problems: Clearly identify and explain the key issues or risks that could affect your game at launch, such as clarity, balance, performance, or player understanding.
Allocate AI and Human Tasks: Decide which parts of the launch and improvement process should be supported by AI and which require human judgement.
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter activity (Slide 7) where students work in pairs to sort four statements about a game in development into 'Problem' or 'Solution' categories, discussing why confusing problems with solutions leads to poor decisions.

In the Explore section (Slide 8), students examine successful and failed game launches. As a mini task, teams choose one successful and one failed game, use AI to summarise player feedback, and analyse it using three guiding questions to identify the main reason for each outcome.

Activity 2.1 (Slides 9–10) is an AI Brainstorming exercise using ideamap.ai. Students follow a three-step process: Step 1 — Generate (use AI to list possible improvements for their game); Step 2 — Filter (cross out weak or irrelevant ideas using human judgement); Step 3 — Decide (select top 3 improvements and explain why they matter). Students use a provided AI prompt template to get suggestions about gameplay, player experience, or clarity improvements.

In the Analyse section (Slide 11), students examine Automation vs. Human Judgement, identifying which launch tasks are AI-Assisted (brainstorming, summarising feedback, generating drafts) versus Human-Led (final decision-making, ethical considerations, creative direction), and discuss the key question: 'Just because AI can do something, should it?'

Activity 2.2 (Slide 12) is the Game Launch Task Allocation exercise, where students work in pairs to sort 12 launch tasks across six categories (Build & Tech, Design, Testing, Communication, Responsibility) into Human, AI, or Shared allocations, writing justifications for each choice to demonstrate responsible AI decision-making.

In the Deepen section (Slide 13), students learn about the Launch Brief as a professional studio planning document used to align teams before release. Portfolio Entry 18 (Slides 14–17) involves students completing a Launch Brief template covering: Launch Problem, Target Players (casual/experienced/first-time gamers, age range), Planned Improvements (up to 3 changes), Success Criteria (measurable signs of success), Game Pillar Review (Mechanics, Design/Dynamics, Narrative/Theme), Testing & Readiness checklist, Launch Campaign (how players will hear about the game), Risk Awareness, and AI vs Human Tasks allocation.

The Reflect section (Slides 18–19) includes an AI Literacy Reflection where students write 2–3 sentences about how AI supported their problem definition using sentence starters ('Today AI helped me by…' / 'I still needed to decide…'), and a 'When to say no' task where students identify one AI suggestion they declined and justify their reasoning by selecting from reasons such as misfit with target players, unrealistic scope, or failure to solve the real problem.

Students complete a self-assessment against the success criteria (Slide 20) using All/Most/Some levels. An extension task (Slide 21) asks students to review their Launch Brief, evaluate where AI provided useful support versus where it would be risky, and write a 'Launch Responsibility Statement' explaining how AI supported decisions while humans remain responsible for the final release.

The lesson concludes with three MCQ Learning Check questions on AI Literacy (Slides 22–24) completed in Classkick, followed by an Endless Studios open build and experimentation session (Slide 25).

Activity 2.1 AI Brainstorm worksheet (top 3 improvements with justifications)
Activity 2.2 Game Launch Task Allocation table (completed with justifications)
AI Literacy Reflection (2–3 sentence written reflection)
Declined AI Suggestion justification ('Why I Rejected This AI-Brainstormed Improvement')
Extension: Launch Responsibility Statement (2–3 sentences)
assessment_signals: Portfolio Entry 18 – Launch Brief (individual planning artifact)
All/Most/Some self-assessment against success criteria (Slide 20)
AI Literacy Reflection (2–3 sentence written reflection using sentence starters)
Declined AI Suggestion justification task — 'Why I Rejected This AI-Brainstormed Improvement' (Slide 19)
MCQ Learning Check questions (Slides 22–24) completed in Classkick
Extension: Responsible AI Launch Review and Launch Responsibility Statement (Slide 21)

lessons.big_question: How do we clearly define what to improve in our game, who it's for, and how we'll know it's successful?
lessons.uae_link: Roblox was banned in the UAE from 2018-2021 due to content and safety concerns, showing why digital products must be carefully planned, reviewed, and aligned with local values before release.
success_criteria: Define Improvement Goals: I can establish realistic, focused launch goals that guide final preparation and measure readiness for the first release.
Clear Problem Definition: Clearly define the game's launch problem(s), their impact on players, and what must be addressed to ensure a successful first release.
I clearly defined a key launch problem and completed a basic Launch Brief.
I differentiated between problems and solutions, set measurable success criteria, and allocate AI vs human tasks appropriately.
I critically evaluated AI limitations and justified which launch decisions must remain human-led.

AQA 3.1.3 (role of software and processes)
IB Criterion C (planning the creation of a solution)
CSTA 3A-CS-02 (decomposing problems within a computing system)

OECD K3.2 — Decide which tasks AI can handle and which need humans (Knowledge Area: AI Reshapes Work & Roles)

Lesson 2: Defining your Launch Strategy

Evaluating game readiness

‹#›

UAE Link: Roblox was banned in the UAE from 2018-2021 due to content and safety concerns, showing why digital products must be carefully planned, reviewed, and aligned with local values before release.
Reflection Question: How can careful planning and responsibility before release help a game avoid problems after launch?

Today you’ll identify what to improve, who your game is for, and how success will be measured. 

Use AI to support planning, 
Decide AI vs human tasks
Create a Launch Brief.

By the end, you’ll have a clear plan to guide your game’s launch and next steps.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Same Prompt, Different Behavior (Slide 7): In pairs, students predict how ChatGPT, a human developer, and an AI agent would each respond to the prompt 'Players are confused in Level 1 — improve the tutorial experience.' No definitions are given yet; students rely on prior understanding.

Portfolio Entry 19A – AI Characteristic Hunt Round 1 (Slide 9): Students identify the AI system type (Rule-Based, Learning-Based AI, Generative AI, or Agentic AI) for seven game-related scenarios and highlight the characteristics that informed their decision. They must justify choices using specific evidence from each scenario.

Portfolio Entry 19B – AI Characteristic Hunt Round 2 (Slides 13–14): In groups, students select one of three scenarios (Launch Review Agent, Bug Sorting Agent, or Feedback Summariser Agent) and complete an Agent Specification template covering: agent goal, tools to use, permitted automatic actions, human-only decisions, when an agent is not needed, and whether to use a single- or multi-agent approach.

Stretch Activity – AI Comparison Table (Slide 15): Students complete a structured table comparing Generative AI (chatbots) and Agentic AI across dimensions including main role, responds or acts, multi-step capability, automatic tool use, human control requirements, and game development examples. Followed by discussion: 'Would you trust either system to make a final launch decision?'

Activity 3.3 – Task Allocation: AI or Human (Slide 18): For four game development scenarios, students decide whether AI, a human, or AI + Human should lead the task and justify their choice using the AI Decision Rule.

Reflection Activities (Slides 19–20): Individual written reflection on which type of AI could support their game project, what decisions stayed in human hands, a final accountability statement, and an AI Literacy Reflection checklist with sentence starters.

Extension – Debug a Bad AI Agent (Slide 22): In pairs or small groups, students analyse a scenario in which a student team's AI Launch Review Agent caused a failed game launch. They identify two problems with the agent design, rewrite one part of the agent specification, and complete an accountability statement.

Learning Check MCQs (Slides 23–25): Three multiple-choice questions (MCQ 3.1, 3.2, 3.3) completed in Classkick, covering AI system types, tasks requiring human judgment, and accurate AI language.

teacher_notes.notes: Welcome students and set the tone for critical thinking about AI. Emphasize that this lesson is about making responsible choices, not just learning definitions. Connect to UAE's vision for responsible technology use. Use the reflection question to gauge prior understanding and concerns. Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-study AQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specification IB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/ CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

Our key question today: When does AI become an agent? Agents act, not just respond. We also consider when using an agent is unnecessary or irresponsible. Keep this question in mind throughout the lesson.

Clearly state the two main learning objectives. Emphasize that understanding AI types is foundational for responsible use. Highlight that decision-making skills are more important than memorizing definitions. Preview how these objectives connect to real-world applications. Curriculum Alignment: Comparing rule-based and learning-based systems aligns with UK D&T KS3 1c (understanding control systems), AQA 3.1.3 (types of systems and limitations), IB Criterion C (justifying design choices), and CSTA 3A-CS-02 (comparing system abstractions and behaviours).

These criteria are measurable and observable. Emphasize the importance of avoiding anthropomorphism - AI doesn't 'think' or 'feel'. Students should be able to justify their decisions with evidence and reasoning. Connect to OECD AI Literacy framework for credibility. Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-study AQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specification IB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/ CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/
Today we explore how the same prompt can lead to different outcomes. ChatGPT gives suggestions as a tool. Humans make final design decisions. AI agents could take repeated actions toward a goal.

AI tool – Learning based AI - https://quickdraw.withgoogle.com/. This is the core conceptual framework for the entire lesson. Use concrete examples for each type (calculator, ChatGPT, autonomous testing bot). Emphasize the key message: more sophisticated ≠ better choice. Check for understanding by asking students to classify additional examples. The three main types of AI systems are AI tools, Generative AI, and Agentic AI. AI tools are rule-based, perform specific tasks when activated, and have no decision-making capability. Examples include calculators and spell checkers. Generative AI can generate text, images, or code, but it responds to user instructions and has no autonomous goals. Examples include ChatGPT and DALL-E. Agentic AI has goals, plans, and autonomy. It pursues objectives independently, plans multiple steps, and uses tools and self-corrects. Examples include autonomous testing bots. The key message is that more powerful AI isn't always the best choice. Selecting the appropriate level of AI capability for each task is important for good design thinking. Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1b-1c: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-curriculum-in-england-design-and-technology-programmes-of-study AQA 3.1.3: https://www.aqa.org.uk/subjects/computer-science-and-it/gcse/computer-science-8525/specification IB Criterion B: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/sciences/computer-science/ CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration): https://csteachers.org/k12standards/

This activity builds understanding of how different AI systems behave, not just what they are called. Students must justify choices using specific characteristics from each scenario. Example Characteristics to Prompt Students: Rule-Based System – Follows fixed IF–THEN rules, does not learn or adapt, same input always gives same output. Learning-Based AI – Improves accuracy over time, uses data and feedback, performance changes as more examples are processed. Generative AI – Creates new content (text, images, music, code), responds to prompts rather than rules, outputs are not pre-written or scripted. Agentic AI – Completes multi-step tasks autonomously, makes decisions and takes actions without constant human input, monitors outcomes and adjusts behaviour. Encourage students to quote or point to evidence in the scenario. Answer Key: A login system blocks access after three failed attempts → Rule-Based (Not AI): fixed security rule, no learning. An AI creates background music to match a game mood → Generative AI: creates new music from prompts. An AI generates concept art from a text description → Generative AI: creates new images from prompts. A spam filter improves accuracy as it processes more data → Machine Learning AI: learns and improves over time. A chatbot answers FAQs using scripted responses → Rule-Based (Not AI): pre-written keyword responses. AI prioritises bugs and creates developer tasks → Agentic AI: makes decisions and automates workflow actions. An AI playtests, fixes bugs, and re-tests automatically → Agentic AI: full autonomous test–fix–repeat loop.

Top section: 'Generative AI responds to prompts — it gives answers, ideas, or text, but it doesn't act on its own and can sound confident even when it's wrong. Agentic AI is goal-driven — it can plan steps, use tools, and work toward an outcome, but it still needs human oversight.' Key distinction to stress: 'Tools answer questions. Agents try to achieve goals.' Single vs Multi-Agent: 'A single agent handles one clear goal and one task flow, like testing a game for bugs. A multi-agent system splits roles — for example, one agent tests, another balances gameplay, another prepares launch feedback.' Big takeaway: 'As agents get more capable, human responsibility increases, not decreases. You always make the final decision.'

Discussion Activity. There is no single correct answer — focus on reasoning, not the choice. Single agent suits small projects: one goal, simpler setup, lower cost, easier control. Multi-agent systems suit complex launches: different agents for testing, marketing, analytics, and updates. Guide students to link their choice to project size, tasks, and responsibility, not 'AI being smarter.' Emphasise that humans remain responsible for final decisions, even when agents assist.

Remind students: Generative AI mainly responds, while AI agents can take actions and complete workflows. Guide them to key distinctions: Chatbots = answer, suggest, create content when asked. Agents = plan, execute steps, use tools, and work toward a goal. Expected answers: Main role: Chatbot supports thinking/content; Agent supports automation/task completion. Responds or acts: Chatbot responds; Agent acts. Across steps: Chatbot usually one-step; Agent can manage multi-step processes. Tools automatically: Chatbot rarely; Agents often can. Human control: Both need oversight, but agents especially because they can trigger real actions. Game dev examples: Chatbot = generate dialogue or brainstorm ideas. Agent = triage bugs, prepare launch checklist, schedule updates. Discussion Prompt Support: Push students to say: Final launch decisions must stay human because AI can miss context, ethics, or real-world consequences.

AI does not think like a human — it predicts patterns from data. Believing it 'thinks' can lead to overtrust and mistakes. AI can be wrong, biased, or misleading if not checked. Responsibility stays with humans: designers, organizations, and users. Key message: AI supports decisions, but people are accountable.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Students choose All, Most, or Some, explain their choice, and record one improvement target. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' Observers should see students articulating progress and next steps using accurate AI language.

Purpose (say briefly): This task checks whether students really understand responsibility, limits, and control when using AI agents — not just how to create one. Set the scene (30 sec): 'Imagine a real studio scenario. The team followed an AI agent's advice exactly… and the launch failed.' Key message to emphasise: AI agents can support decisions, but they do not replace human judgement. During the task: Encourage students to focus on why the agent failed, not blaming the AI. Listen for phrases like: 'The agent had too much control', 'Humans didn't review the decision', 'The goal was too vague'. Prompt if groups get stuck: 'What should the agent not be allowed to do?' 'Where should a human step back in?'

AI inputs, instructions, and outputs as determinants of reliability
System settings and operational limits for AI
Source-grounded and citation-based AI responses

metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Think-Pair-Share (Slide 6): Students consider what causes AI errors, choosing one of four causes (the AI system itself, the information it was given, the way the question was asked, a human trusting it too quickly), connecting it to a real example from school or game design, and sharing their reasoning with the class.

Activity 4.1 – Convert + Load the Data (Slide 8): Students navigate to NotebookLM and create a notebook for their Game Development Project. They upload key documents including their Launch Brief, screenshots or photos of their game, Term 2 and Term 3 portfolios, playtest feedback, and bug lists. PowerPoint files must be saved as PDFs before uploading. Students then identify what information the AI can now access and what was forgotten or missing.

Activity 4.2 – Create Launch Review Agent (Slides 12–13): Students upload their Launch Brief and supporting documents (Term 2 + Term 3 Portfolio, initial Design Brief, playtest feedback/bug list) to NotebookLM. They configure an agent using the provided Agent Role Template (Launch Review Agent), ask the agent to review their launch plan, and answer 4 final questions. Students apply 4 MUST-PASS Criteria (game is playable start to finish, no game-breaking bugs, core mechanic works as intended, game has been tested by at least one person) to make a GO or CONDITIONAL launch decision, and write 1–2 sentences justifying where they agreed or disagreed with the agent.

Activity 4.3 – Multi-Source Review Agent (Slide 17): Students upload their initial Design Brief and Term 2 + Term 3 portfolios to a NotebookLM workspace. Using a Development Review Agent with specific instructions (what matched the original plan, what changed, what improved through playtesting, what still needs work), they compare their final game to the original design. Students write one paragraph explaining how their game evolved and identify one improvement they would still make before launch.

Extension Task – Output Formats in NotebookLM (Slide 20): Students upload their project portfolio to NotebookLM and use the Video Overview feature to select a brief video overview and generate a 1-minute video-style summary of their game project using a provided example prompt.

Reflect – Controlling AI, Owning Decisions (Slides 22–23): Students complete structured reflection prompts: What Enabled AI (what they uploaded and what it allowed the AI to do), What Limited the AI (what was missing or unclear and how it affected output), and Your Decision (one decision that required their own judgement, values, or game understanding). Students also complete an AI Literacy Reflection checklist and write 2–3 sentences on the AI's role versus their own role.

Endless Studios Session (Slide 28): Open build session where students build and experiment in Endstar, test with their team, fix bugs together, try new ideas, and explore AI tools.

They upload key documents including their Launch Brief, screenshots or photos of their game, Term 2 and Term 3 portfolios, playtest feedback, and bug lists. PowerPoint files must be saved as PDFs before uploading. Students then identify what information the AI can now access and what was forgotten or missing.

Activity 4.2 – Create Launch Review Agent (Slides 12–13): Students upload their Launch Brief and supporting documents (Term 2 + Term 3 Portfolio, initial Design Brief, playtest feedback/bug list) to NotebookLM. They configure an agent using the provided Agent Role Template (Launch Review Agent), ask the agent to review their launch plan, and answer 4 final questions. Students apply 4 MUST-PASS Criteria (game is playable start to finish, no game-breaking bugs, core mechanic works as intended, game has been tested by at least one person) to make a GO or CONDITIONAL launch decision, and write 1–2 sentences justifying where they agreed or disagreed with the agent.

Activity 4.3 – Multi-Source Review Agent (Slide 17): Students upload their initial Design Brief and Term 2 + Term 3 portfolios to a NotebookLM workspace. Using a Development Review Agent with specific instructions (what matched the original plan, what changed, what improved through playtesting, what still needs work), they compare their final game to the original design. Students write one paragraph explaining how their game evolved and identify one improvement they would still make before launch.

Extension Task – Output Formats in NotebookLM (Slide 20): Students upload their project portfolio to NotebookLM and use the Video Overview feature to select a brief video overview and generate a 1-minute video-style summary of their game project using a provided example prompt.

Reflect – Controlling AI, Owning Decisions (Slides 22–23): Students complete structured reflection prompts: What Enabled AI (what they uploaded and what it allowed the AI to do), What Limited the AI (what was missing or unclear and how it affected output), and Your Decision (one decision that required their own judgement, values, or game understanding). Students also complete an AI Literacy Reflection checklist and write 2–3 sentences on the AI's role versus their own role.

Endless Studios Session (Slide 28): Open build session where students build and experiment in Endstar, test with their team, fix bugs together, try new ideas, and explore AI tools.

teacher_notes.notes: Lesson 4 focuses on building a controlled AI workspace and learning how to manage an AI agent responsibly. Students will understand that strong AI performance depends on how well the workspace is structured and governed. Introduce the three key components that determine AI reliability: Inputs, Instructions, and Outputs. Explain that AI can only respond based on what it is given, so poor inputs lead to poor or misleading results. Highlight that clear instructions reduce confusion and help keep the agent within safe boundaries. Outputs must be checked by humans, with evidence and reasoning, not accepted automatically. Connect to the UAE's national AI strategy, where trusted data and governance are essential in high-impact sectors like healthcare and education. Use the reflection question to prompt critical thinking: incorrect or incomplete information can lead to serious consequences in learning, business, or public communication.
Guiding Computational Thinking Question: What if we don't have the right tools — how can we adapt and problem-solve in Endstar?

*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion C | CSTA 3A-CS-02 (Computing Systems)* Students first choose one cause they believe is most likely. In pairs, they connect it to a real example from schoolwork or their game development project. Students then share their reasoning with the class to highlight different perspectives. Reinforce the key message: most AI errors come from unclear instructions, biased data, or humans trusting too quickly — not because AI is "thinking" like a person.

Students will turn their Launch Brief into a Launch Review Agent using NotebookLM. The agent's role is to evaluate launch readiness, not to invent new features or decisions. The agent should review across Design, QA/Test, and Launch Readiness. Remind students: the agent must only use information from the uploaded brief. Students upload the Launch Brief, apply the role template, and run a launch review prompt. The agent should flag risks, missing details, unclear goals, and possible bias/assumptions. Students then apply the 4 MUST-PASS Criteria to make the final GO or CONDITIONAL decision. Students write 1–2 sentences explaining where they agreed or disagreed with the agent and why.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Students choose All, Most, or Some, explain their choice, and record one improvement target. Ask: "What will you do differently next time?" Observers should see students articulating progress and next steps using accurate AI language.

metadata.title: Launch Announcement Creation
core_topics: AI-assisted writing and responsible AI use
Human judgment and accountability over AI output
UAE AI Ethics Guidelines on transparency and accountability
ai_focus: AI-assisted writing and drafting (Gemini, Notebook LM)
Responsible and ethical AI use in marketing
Human judgment and oversight over AI-generated content
AI capabilities and limitations in understanding ethics and player trust
AI accountability — humans are responsible for AI-generated claims
Prompt engineering for tagline generation
AI as a drafting tool, not a decision-maker
learning_objectives: Apply Ethical Judgment: Evaluate marketing language to ensure it is accurate, fair, and does not overpromise beyond the game's actual experience.
Structured Launch Announcement: Create a clear launch announcement using a provided template that communicates what the game is and who it is for.
metadata.activity_type: mixed — individual creation (launch announcement drafting and ethical review) with pair work (peer review exchange and partner discussions)
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — Launch Decision Recap (Slide 7): Students recall their Lesson 4 launch decision (clean or conditional launch), identify one known issue if conditional, and name one claim they must be honest about. They reflect that AI can help write but students own the promises.

Explore — What Is a Launch Announcement? (Slide 8): Whole-class discussion on what a launch announcement promises players. Students share examples of misleading game marketing, discuss how it felt when games didn't match trailers, and identify the key purposes of an honest launch announcement: telling players what the game truly offers, attracting the right audience, highlighting real features, and building trust.

Explore — Game Tagline Importance (Slide 9): Students learn that a tagline must match the real game. Discussion covers what makes a tagline memorable, what it should signal about gameplay, and the risks of overpromising.

Activity 5.1 — Launch Announcement Agent (Slide 10): Students open their previous Launch Review Agent Notebook in Notebook LM, paste the Launch Announcement Agent Prompt, choose or create a tagline from the generated options, review the ethical check for honesty and accuracy, and save the final announcement draft as a note. A video tutorial is available for support.

Analyse — Ethical Marketing Check (Slide 11): Before finalising their announcement, students conduct an ethical review by answering three questions: Does the tagline match the real game? Is the description honest? Would they proudly post this today? Students write one change they will make and explain why.

Activity 5.1: Step 1 — AI-Supported Tagline Drafting (Slide 12): Students open Gemini, copy a provided example prompt (replacing placeholders with their game details from the Launch Brief), generate 3 tagline options, cross out any that overpromise, and select one tagline to keep or edit.

Activity 5.1: Step 2 — Complete the Launch Announcement (Slides 13–14): Students download the Launch Announcement Template and complete three sections over approximately 18–21 minutes: AI-supported tagline drafting (~5–6 mins), filling in all announcement fields (Game Title, Tagline, What the Game Is, Who It's For; Call to Action left blank for Lesson 6) (~10 mins), and an ethical check to verify no overpromising or misleading claims (~3–5 mins). Students using conditional launches must not hide known issues.

Portfolio Entry 22 — Launch Announcement Draft (Slide 15): Students save their final announcement as a note in Notebook LM and add screenshots of all three sections (tagline, announcement, ethical check). They record their tagline selection and one improvement identified through the ethical check.

Stretch Activity — Tagline Variation Challenge (Slide 16): Students create three versions of their tagline, each targeting a different ethical concern, then discuss with a partner which they would actually use and why, considering how word choice impacts player expectations.

Deepen — AI in Marketing: Help or Risk? (Slide 17): Partner discussion and analysis of a comparison table showing how AI helps (generating options quickly, saving time, suggesting phrasings) versus why humans must decide (only humans know if the game matches the promise, humans are responsible for what gets promised, player trust depends on human judgment). Students identify one claim they must personally verify before posting.

Extension Task — Peer Review Exchange (Slide 18): Students pair up, exchange launch announcements, read as a potential player, and provide written feedback on clarity, accuracy, and audience fit. Students use Gemini to generate three follow-up questions about their partner's target audience, then discuss feedback and AI-generated questions together.

Reflection — Ethical Release Decisions (Slide 19): Students complete a structured written reflection in three parts: (1) Accuracy & Honesty — identify one claim that could mislead players and provide real gameplay evidence for it; (2) Ethical Responsibility — select the correct statement about their responsibility when using AI for marketing and explain their choice; (3) Public Accountability — identify what part of the announcement they would be accountable for if a player felt disappointed.

Learning Check — MCQs (Slides 21–23): Students complete three multiple-choice questions in Classkick to practise for the end-of-unit assessment, covering human responsibility over AI-generated content (K3.3), appropriate tasks for AI support in marketing (K4.1), and why humans must make the final marketing decision (K5.4).

lessons.big_question: How can we promote our game clearly and honestly without misleading players?
lessons.uae_link: This lesson connects to UAE AI Ethics Guidelines on transparency and accountability in digital communication. Honest marketing builds trust, a core value in the UAE's Vision 2031 for digital innovation.
success_criteria: Evidence of Ethical Review: Review our marketing ethically to ensure accuracy, honesty, and realistic expectations.
Complete Launch Announcement: Create a full launch announcement, including a title, tagline, description, and target audience.
All: I can create a clear launch announcement that accurately describes the game and target audience.
Most: I can evaluate and refine marketing language to ensure it is honest, realistic, and ethically responsible.
Some: I can critically justify marketing choices and defend how their announcement protects player trust and accountability.

metadata.title: Launch / Release Day
core_topics: Responsible digital publishing and launch announcements
Game website creation and hosting on itch.io
AI literacy: human judgment, AI capabilities and limits, AI authorship
ai_focus: Using Gemini to generate AI platform recommendations based on game type and goals
Critical evaluation of AI recommendations — AI suggests, humans decide
Using Claude.ai to generate game landing page HTML code from a structured template
Using NotebookLM Launch Review Agent findings as input for game description and reflection
AI literacy: human judgment must override AI advice based on context and player impact (MCQ 6.1)
AI capabilities and limits: AI relies on past patterns, not student-specific readiness (MCQ 6.2)
AI authorship and accountability: humans are responsible for decisions made using AI support (MCQ 6.3)
Responsible use of AI tools in game publishing, marketing, and distribution decisions
learning_objectives: Evaluate Publishing Platforms: Compare game platforms and critically assess an AI recommendation to plan future distribution.
Execute a Responsible Game Release: Publish your game to Endstar, create a working deeplink, and post an accurate launch announcement that reflects their launch decision.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter (Slide 6): Students discuss player expectations through a connection task called 'Would You Click This?' — they consider two game descriptions (one honest, one exaggerated) and vote on which sets more honest expectations, opening a discussion on how players judge games before clicking.

Activity 6.1 — Publishing your Game on EndStar (Slides 8–10): Students follow a step-by-step video tutorial to publish their game. Steps include: opening EndStar and searching for their game, taking screenshots for level and game cover photos, adding a Level Title, Level Description, and thumbnail, opening the Game Editor to add a Game Title, Game Thumbnail, and Game Description (pasting their Final Launch Announcement from NotebookLM), clicking Publish to select a version, returning to the Lobby to find their published game, and copying the Share Link (deeplink).

Portfolio Entry 23 (Slide 12): Students add a screenshot of their Published Launch Announcement, paste their EndStar deeplink, shorten it using a URL shortener tool, and record the shortened URL.

Analyze (Slide 13): Group discussion (2–3 min) where students share what game publishing platforms they already know.

Platform Exploration (Slides 14–16): Teacher-led overview of three platforms — Itch.io (indie-focused, 0% default cut, HTML5 browser support), Steam (120M monthly users, $100 listing fee, 30% cut), and Endstar (no approval, deeplink sharing, portfolio-building focus).

Deepen (Slide 18): Teacher-led overview of self-publishing vs publisher partnerships, covering trade-offs in creative control, revenue, and marketing responsibilities.

Activity 6.2 — Future Platform Planning (Slides 19–20): Students open their Future Platform Slide template, compare Itch.io, Steam, and Endstar, use Gemini AI to generate a platform recommendation based on their game and goals, then critically evaluate the AI's suggestion and justify their own final decision. Portfolio Entry 24 is the completed Future Platform Slide.

Stretch Task (Slide 17): Students generate a game landing website using Claude.ai and a provided website building template. Steps: paste the template and Launch Review Agent findings into Claude.ai, answer questions in chat, download the generated index.html, build a local folder structure (GameLandingPage/index.html + images/), test locally, zip and upload to itch.io for browser-playable hosting.

Reflection (Slide 21): Students reflect on their Lesson 4 launch decision, writing 1–2 sentences for each of four questions: what the Launch Review Agent found that they agreed with, what they overrode, whether they published with known issues, and their plan to fix them.

Learning Check (Slides 24–26): Three MCQ questions (MCQ 6.1–6.3) on AI literacy, completed in Classkick. Topics: human judgment vs AI advice (K3.3), AI's limitations as pattern-based reasoning (K4.1), and authorship/accountability when using AI (K5.3).

Homework (Slide 22): Students share their Endstar link with 1–2 people outside class, collect structured playtest feedback using a provided feedback form, and bring completed forms to Lesson 7.

Portfolio Entry 24: Future Platform Slide with platform comparison table, AI recommendation, and justified final decision
Published Endstar game with working deeplink
Game landing page website (index.html hosted on itch.io) — stretch task
Reflection responses on launch decisions (Slide 21)
Playtest feedback forms collected from external players (homework for Lesson 7)

MCQ 6.2 — AI capabilities and limits: pattern vs reasoning (Classkick; OECD K4.1; answer: A)
MCQ 6.3 — AI authorship and accountability (Classkick; OECD K5.3; answer: B)
End-of-lesson self-assessment: All/Most/Some criteria with one improvement target recorded
Homework: Structured playtest feedback collected from external players using provided feedback form

metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Students engage in a Think–Pair–Share discussion on why players notice problems the development team missed. They reflect on positive feedback they hope to receive and criticism they fear, then share with the class. Key idea reinforced: once a game is public, developers must listen — not defend.

Explore (Slide 7): Students review why playtest feedback matters — finding gameplay problems early, making evidence-based improvements, and understanding what players enjoy. They discuss what surprised them during their Term 2 playtest.

Activity 7.1 – In-Class Playtest Feedback: Students share their Endstar deeplink with 3–4 classmates and play each other's games for 5–7 minutes each. Each tester completes a 6-question Playtest Feedback Form and saves it with a unique filename (e.g., FeedbackMohammed_SpaceRunner.docx). The target is at least 4 completed forms total, combining in-class and homework testers.

Activity 7.2 (Part 1) – Preparing Feedback for Analysis: Students upload all completed feedback forms and supporting project documents (Launch Brief, Launch Review Agent Findings, Term 2 + Term 3 Portfolio, Playtest Feedback Forms) to their NotebookLM workspace. They then perform a 2–3 minute human scan to identify surprising comments, strong reactions, and memorable player quotes.

Activity 7.2 (Part 2) – Social Media Posts from Feedback: Students ask NotebookLM for the top 5 positive player quotes, select 2 strong quotes or feedback themes, upload a gameplay screenshot to ChatGPT, and use a provided prompt template to generate social media post images. They complete this for both chosen quotes and save each generated image for their portfolio.

Extension Task – Feedback Pattern Investigator (optional, for fast finishers): Students ask NotebookLM to group feedback into themes (e.g., 'confusing start,' 'fun mechanic,' 'too difficult,' 'slow pacing'), identify one repeating pattern and one surprising comment, decide what change to make, and reflect on why that decision belongs to the developer — not the AI.

Reflection – Feedback Blind Spots: Students complete a 2–3 minute individual reflection table identifying what surprised them in the feedback, evidence from testers, why they missed it before playtesting, and what they learned. They also answer a question about what AI could help with versus what only a human can decide.

Self-Assessment: Students assess whether they met All, Most, or Some of today's success criteria and identify one improvement target.

Learning Check: Students answer three MCQ questions in Classkick on AI literacy — covering human judgment in AI recommendations (MCQ 7.1, answer C), sampling bias in AI analysis (MCQ 7.2, answer B), and pattern recognition vs. emotional understanding in AI (MCQ 7.3, answer A).

metadata.title: Feedback Analysis & Iteration Planning
core_topics: AI literacy and responsible AI use
ai_focus: NotebookLM for AI-assisted feedback analysis
AI pattern detection vs human decision-making
Evaluating and critically reviewing AI outputs
Bias in AI outputs (overweighting repeated complaints)
Human accountability for AI-assisted decisions
Responsible use of AI in design workflows
AI literacy: knowing when to follow, adapt, or reject AI recommendations
learning_objectives: Build Evidence-Based Plans: Create a clear, prioritised improvement plan using real player feedback and explain the reasons behind your decisions.
Analyse Feedback with AI: Use AI to review playtest feedback and apply your own judgment to improve your game.
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter activity (2 mins, pairs) titled 'Feedback ≠ Instructions', in which students discuss whether developers should fix all player feedback and how AI can help sort feedback while humans retain decision authority. Students consider problems from fixing every complaint versus ignoring feedback entirely.

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Analysis with NotebookLM (15 mins): Students open NotebookLM with feedback forms from Lesson 7 and copy a provided agent prompt to categorise all playtest feedback as Positive, Negative, or Suggestion, cross-referencing against the Launch Brief's top 3 problems and Launch Review Agent findings. Students critically review the AI output by checking for match accuracy, missing ideas, and potential bias from repeated complaints. They agree on and record 3–5 feedback themes and save the analysis as a Note in NotebookLM.

Deepen – Building an Iteration Plan (20 mins, Portfolio Entry 26): Students create a prioritised Iteration Plan using four evidence sources: feedback analysis from NotebookLM, launch review findings from Lesson 4, gap analysis, and known launch issues. They list 5–8 improvement issues in a provided table template, mark top 1–2 priorities with a star, and explain their reasoning independently of AI suggestions.

Extension Task – Iteration Plan Peer Review (5–7 mins, pairs): Students swap Iteration Plans, evaluate the priorities against the justifications, identify one missing or over-prioritised issue, and provide one written suggestion for improvement. Students then note one change to make in their own plan.

Reflection – Choosing My Next Fix (3–5 mins, individual): Students identify their #1 improvement for Lesson 9, explain their reasoning with evidence, and reflect on whether they followed, adapted, or rejected the AI's recommendation, articulating why human decision-making is important in game design.

Self-Assessment: Students assess their own progress against All/Most/Some success criteria and record an improvement target.

Learning Check: Students answer 3 AI Literacy MCQs (8.1, 8.2, 8.3) in Classkick, focusing on responsible use of AI feedback analysis, bias in AI outputs, and accountability in AI-assisted decision-making.

core_topics: Second-order effects in connected game systems

Responsible use of AI suggestions — humans must verify, not accept wholesale

learning_objectives: Evaluate Fix Effectiveness: Update your plan with clear evidence showing whether fixes were successful, partial, or still unresolved.
Implement Evidence-Based Improvements: Apply top-priority fixes from your Iteration Plan and test them effectively.
metadata.activity_type: mixed — primarily individual build/fix time with pair discussion in starters and whole-class share
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with two starter activities. 'AI Discovery Warm-Up' (Slide 6) uses a Think-Pair-Share structure (5 mins total): students silently consider which game tasks benefit most from AI versus which still need human control, then pair up to agree on one example of each, before sharing with the class. 'Fix the Right Thing' (Slide 7) is a 5-minute team task in which students analyse a scenario of 10 pieces of player feedback (e.g., 3 players report slow controls, 2 say they don't understand the objective, 1 suggests multiplayer) and determine the single top-priority fix, distinguishing between fixing a real problem and adding a new feature. A stretch segment (Slide 8) references a video showing Claude integrated with Blender to support 3D creative production workflows.

The 'Explore' segment (Slide 9) contextualises the lesson: students move from Lesson 8 planning to execution, with a strict rule of fixing only 1–2 priority issues rather than everything. The 'Analyze' segment (Slide 10) walks through the implementation and immediate testing protocol step by step: make only the necessary change, play the exact section affected, test at least twice for consistent behaviour, and watch for unintended side effects.

Activity 9.1 – Implement Top-Priority Fixes (Slide 11, 25 mins): Students open their Iteration Plan from Lesson 8, select 1–2 starred top priorities, implement each fix in their game, immediately play-test the affected section, verify the issue is resolved, and update the Iteration Plan with status, brief test evidence, and reassessed priority. The instruction is explicit: fix → test → verify, no batching.

The 'Deepen' segment (Slide 12) teaches second-order effects: fixing one issue in a connected game system can create new bugs, change difficulty, inadvertently resolve another issue, or produce positive side effects. Students are prompted to reassess remaining priorities after every successful fix.

Activity 9.2 – Review Remaining Items (Slide 13, 5 mins): Students review every unresolved item in their Iteration Plan and categorise each as 'Still Relevant' (carries forward to Lesson 12 Scope Freeze), 'Now Resolved / No Longer Relevant' (mark closed), or 'Lower Priority' (adjust ranking).

Portfolio Entry 27 – Updated Iteration Plan (Slide 14): Students update their existing iteration table with three new columns: Status After Testing (Done / Partial / Still Open), Evidence / Test Result (short, specific), and Revised Priority (Still Relevant / Lower Priority / No Longer Relevant). An example row is provided showing a completed fix for a missing start button.

Reflection – Did My Fixes Actually Work? (Slide 15): Part 1 asks students to identify their most important fix, describe how they tested it, select whether it fully/partly/did not solve the problem, and state the evidence. Part 2 asks students to select applicable testing lessons learned (e.g., 'evidence must be documented', 'fixes can create new issues') and explain their choice.

Extension (Slide 16, 10 mins): Students who properly finished all priority fixes choose one additional unresolved item from their Iteration Plan, implement and immediately test the fix, and update the three tracking columns. Quality over speed is emphasised.

The lesson closes with a self-assessment slide (Slide 17) using All/Most/Some criteria, followed by three AI literacy MCQ questions (Slides 18–20) completed in Classkick, covering responsible verification of AI suggestions and the limits of AI in confirming bug fix effectiveness.

metadata.title: Product-Market Fit
ai_focus: AI capabilities and limitations in evaluating Product-Market Fit
Human judgment vs AI decision-making
AI pattern detection without context or goal understanding
Critical evaluation of AI-generated feedback summaries
Human accountability for decisions made with AI support
Use of Gemini for research and manual verification
Launch Review Agent from L4
AI idea generation vs decision ownership
learning_objectives: Build Evidence-Based Conclusions: Use feedback analysis and iteration results to evaluate whether your game fits its target audience.
Apply the PMF Framework: Understand what Product–Market Fit means and why it requires real evidence.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter (Slide 7): Students rank six success metrics (downloads, revenue, retention, reviews, recommendations, playtime) from most to least important and write a sentence justifying their top choice. Discussion questions explore whether high downloads or revenue alone equals success.

Explore (Slides 8–9): Teacher-led introduction to Product–Market Fit (PMF). Students learn that PMF cannot be proven before launch and requires real player data, observable behavior, authentic feedback, and measurable engagement — not opinions or AI predictions. A five-stage process (assumptions → MVP → feedback → iterate → assess fit) mirrors Lessons 6–10.

Activity 10.1 – Identify Product–Market Fit Signals (Slides 10–11): Students select a favorite game and complete a structured PMF analysis covering Target Audience, Core Need, Love Signals, Retention Evidence, and Unique Value. They classify the game as Strong Fit, Partial Fit, or Weak Fit with a written justification (5 mins).

Analyze – PMF in the Real World (Slide 12): Class discussion examining a real game example through the PMF lens — audience, core need, and evidence of fit.

PMF Checklist Overview (Slide 14): Teacher introduces five evidence types students will use — Target Audience Match, Satisfaction Signals, Pain Points, Retention Signal, and USP Clarity — drawing from L2 Launch Brief, L8 Feedback Analysis, L9 Iteration Plan, and Playtest Feedback Forms.

Portfolio Entry 28 – PMF Evidence Checklist (Slide 15): Students complete a structured checklist (25 mins) using real data from their game development: target audience match, 2–3 positive player quotes, pain points addressed in L9 iteration, retention numbers, and USP confirmation from testers.

Deepen – AI vs Human Decision Making (Slide 16): Discussion on whether AI should determine PMF. Students consider the Launch Review Agent from L4, raw player feedback, and what emotional or creative signals humans detect that AI cannot.

Reflection (Slide 17): Students classify their own game as Strong Fit, Partial Fit, or Not Yet, justify with evidence, identify their strongest and weakest PMF signal, and consider whether their evidence would convince a real investor.

Extension – Real PMF Case Study (Slide 18, early finishers only): Students research one indie game that achieved PMF and one that failed. Step 1: Use Gemini to investigate audience, success/failure reasons, reviews, and retention. Step 2: Manually verify findings via Steam reviews, articles, and interviews. Step 3: Compare both games using the PMF lens and classify each.

Learning Check (Slides 20–22): Three AI literacy MCQs completed in Classkick, covering why AI alone cannot determine PMF (K3.3), responsible use of AI-generated feedback summaries (K3.3), and AI pattern detection vs human interpretation (K4.1).

metadata.activity_type: mixed — primarily individual with pair peer review
metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Hope vs Evidence (Slide 6, 5 mins): Students individually decide their game's PMF level (Strong Fit, Partial Fit, or No Fit), justify in 1–2 sentences using at least one number, and reflect on potential biases (focusing on positives, small sample size, enjoyment ≠ retention). An example scenario is provided: 10 testers played the game; 5 said they enjoyed it, 5 said they would not play again.

Activity 11.1 – Finalize Your PMF Evidence Checklist (Slide 8, 8 mins): Students open L10 PMF Evidence Checklist, L9 Updated Iteration Plan, and L8 Feedback Analysis (NotebookLM) side by side. Step 1: Update with fix results from L9 (did satisfaction improve? were pain points reduced or removed? did retention signals change?). Step 2: Strengthen evidence by replacing vague statements with real quotes, numbers (e.g., '7/10 would play again'), and specific examples. Step 3: Fill any gaps across all five categories (Target Audience, Satisfaction, Pain Points, Retention, Unique Value). Goal: checklist clearly shows consistent evidence of PMF — or not.

Activity 11.2 – Using NotebookLM for PMF Assessment (Slide 10): Students open their NotebookLM workbook with all project files uploaded, upload their Updated PMF Checklist and Iteration Plan, and paste the sample prompt: 'Based on all playtest feedback, iteration results, and the PMF Evidence Checklist—does my game have product–market fit? Summarize evidence for and against.' Students critically evaluate the response (is it fair and balanced? what context does it miss? what developer insight does AI lack?) then write one paragraph choosing Strong, Partial, or Weak PMF, justified with real evidence — not just the AI's opinion.

Portfolio Entry 29 – PMF Evidence Checklist with Conclusion (Slide 11): Students perform a quick evidence check (5–8 mins), updating the L10 checklist with L9 fix results, adding missing numbers or quotes, and ensuring retention % is accurate. Students then write a PMF Conclusion Paragraph justifying their chosen PMF level with specific quotes, numbers, and iteration results.

Activity 11.3 – Generate Your Opener with Gemini (Slide 13): Students use a provided prompt template to generate a 30-second showcase opener in Gemini ('I'm pitching [GAME TITLE], a [GENRE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here's my PMF conclusion: [paste your conclusion]. Write a 30-second showcase opener that explains: what's the game, who's it for, why they should care, and includes one piece of evidence from my PMF work.'). metadata.title: Scope Freeze & Trailer Pre-Production
ai_focus: Decision governance: AI informs, humans decide
Human vs. AI accountability for project decisions
ME / AI / BOTH decision ownership audit and self-audit
NotebookLM for PMF analysis and trailer pre-production planning
Suno for AI-generated game soundtrack prompts
ElevenLabs for AI-generated voiceover lines
Gemini for trailer plan stress-testing and optimization
Evaluating and accepting or rejecting AI output critically
AI pattern recognition vs. contextual reasoning (OECD K4.1)
AI authorship and creative ownership (OECD K5.3)
learning_objectives: Plan Creative Assets Strategically — Design a complete trailer and poster pre-production plan — before any recording or editing begins.
Apply Professional Scope Control — Use PMF evidence to categorize every remaining issue as DONE, FIX, or CUT — with clear justification.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter – Reflection Task (3 mins): Students answer one question based on their PMF result from Lesson 11. Strong PMF teams identify one improvement to make their game undeniable; Partial PMF teams identify the one gap stopping them from Strong PMF; Weak PMF teams identify the one change that would get testers to say 'I'd play again.' This reflection directly feeds into the Scope Freeze.

Activity 12.1 – Scope Freeze Decision Table (10–12 mins): Students open their L9 Updated Iteration Plan and L11 PMF Conclusion. For each remaining issue or feature, they make a DONE / FIX / CUT decision, recording PMF evidence (quotes, numbers, results) and noting 'Who Decided?' (ME / AI / BOTH). FIX is limited to max 1–2 small items. Students are challenged to be ruthless and evidence-based in their categorisation.

Activity 12.2 – Self-Audit (3 mins): Students count their ME / AI / BOTH totals from the Scope Freeze table and write a 1–2 sentence reflection on their decision ownership. A class show-of-hands follows, connecting to OECD AI literacy framework (K3.3 and K4.1).

Activity 12.3 – Trailer Pre-Production Plan (15–18 mins): Students download the Trailer Pre-Production Plan Template (or use NotebookLM with a system configuration prompt) and complete four sections: Suno Prompt (2–3 versions covering mood, genre, energy), Voice Plan (1–2 lines + tone), Shot List (Hook | Gameplay | End Card), and Poster Plan (Title | Tagline | Visual | Quote). Students screenshot each section and add to Portfolio Entry 31.

Stretch – Trailer Optimization Challenge: Students upload their completed Trailer Pre-Production Plan screenshots to Gemini with a prompt asking for 2 improvements for pacing and audience clarity, then make and justify a professional Accept or Reject decision in full sentences.

Homework: Students implement their 1–2 'FIX' items before L13, play-test twice (with one quick tester check if possible), and record 3–4 sentences of evidence covering what the issue was, what changed, and whether it improved the game.

teacher_notes.notes: Use this slide to open a short whole-class discussion. Ask: "Has anyone ever kept tweaking something and made it worse?" Draw the connection between over-engineering and loss of clarity. Emphasize that constraints are a professional superpower, not a limitation. Spend 3–5 minutes here before moving to objectives.

Curriculum Alignment – Lesson 12: Scope Freeze & Finalisation
Implementing a structured scope freeze and making final DONE / FIX / CUT decisions aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and improving designs through iteration and informed decision-making), AQA 3.1.1 (evaluating and refining digital products using user feedback and performance data), IB Design Criterion C & D (creating solutions and evaluating their success against explicit criteria), and CSTA 3A-AP-17 (systematically testing, refining, and finalising computational artifacts).

Explain scope freeze as a formal, intentional decision — not just "stopping." Use the analogy of a restaurant menu: chefs don't add new dishes on the night of a dinner service. Ask: "When does building become a way of avoiding launching?" Pause for responses. This concept may feel counterintuitive to students who love adding features — acknowledge that tension directly.

AI as a production accelerator with humans as creative directors
Evaluating and selecting AI-generated outputs critically
OECD AI literacy framework — K3.3 human judgment, K5.4 accountability

metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter: Trailer Hook Challenge. Students watch the first 10 seconds of the Fortnite Official Trailer and complete a Think-Pair-Share activity, identifying one main reason the hook is effective (gameplay moment, music, pace, visual impact, or energy), then sharing one unique reason per pair.

Activity 13.1 – Homework Bug Verification: Students launch their game with the homework bug fixes applied, directly test the fixed issues, confirm whether each issue is resolved, and record status (Working or Still Bugged). This is a rapid verification check — no debugging. If a fix failed, students document it and move on.

Activity 13.2 – Gameplay Capture (12 mins): Students open their L12 Shot List and record three essential gameplay moments: (1) Hook Moment — the first 3 visually arresting seconds; (2) Core Mechanic — a clear demonstration of the game's unique feature; (3) Visual Highlight — one strong or exciting aesthetic moment. Students must record clean footage with no glitches, re-record if needed, save all takes, and name files exactly as specified in the project folder.

Activity 13.3 – Suno AI Soundtrack Workflow: Students copy their existing NotebookLM L12 prompt directly into Suno AI without rewriting it, generate 2–3 versions, critically evaluate each against their game's tone and hook moment, refine by changing one small detail if needed, and export the best version as Soundtrack_Final.mp3.

Activity 13.4 – Trailer Voiceover with ElevenLabs: Students paste their 1–2 voiceover lines from their L12 plan into ElevenLabs Text to Speech, select a voice matching their game's tone, adjust speed/stability/clarity sliders if needed, generate 2 versions, evaluate critically for naturalness and audience fit, and export the strongest version as Voiceover_Final.mp3.

Stretch Task – AI Trailer Visual Enhancement: Students use Meshy AI to create a cinematic version of an existing character, boss, prop, or iconic object from their game. They sketch or screenshot the original, upload to Meshy for a refined 3D version, compare both side by side, and write a one-sentence reflection on authenticity. Evidence required: original screenshot, Meshy version, and reflection.

Reflection – Human Creativity Check: Students write 3 specific examples of elements in their game that are uniquely human — mechanics inspired by personal experience, dialogue reflecting personality, design choices from personal taste, problems solved creatively, or emotions intended for players — explaining why AI could not generate each without knowing them.

Extension Task – Alternative Asset Exploration: Students return to Suno AI and generate an alternative soundtrack with a deliberately different genre, tempo, or mood (using a modified prompt), then return to ElevenLabs and apply a different voice style to the same voiceover lines. Students compare both pairs of assets and write a one-sentence justification for their primary selection based on target audience fit.

Gameplay footage — Core Mechanic clip

Voiceover_Final.mp3 — ElevenLabs generated voiceover

Reflection: 3 written examples of uniquely human creative contributions
Meshy AI cinematic visual asset with authenticity reflection (stretch task)
Alternative soundtrack and voiceover comparison with written justification (extension task)

MCQ 13.2 (Classkick) — OECD K5.4: revising AI voiceover that misrepresents gameplay features

Extension Task: written one-sentence justification for primary audio asset selection based on target audience fit

lessons.detailed_activities:
The lesson opens with a Starter: Trailer Hook Challenge. Students watch the first 10 seconds of the Fortnite Official Trailer and complete a Think-Pair-Share activity, identifying one main reason the hook is effective (gameplay moment, music, pace, visual impact, or energy), then sharing one unique reason per pair.

Activity 13.1 – Homework Bug Verification: Students launch their game with the homework bug fixes applied, directly test the fixed issues, confirm whether each issue is resolved, and record status (Working or Still Bugged). This is a rapid verification check — no debugging. If a fix failed, students document it and move on.

Activity 13.2 – Gameplay Capture (12 mins): Students open their L12 Shot List and record three essential gameplay moments: (1) Hook Moment — the first 3 visually arresting seconds; (2) Core Mechanic — a clear demonstration of the game's unique feature; (3) Visual Highlight — one strong or exciting aesthetic moment. Students must record clean footage with no glitches, re-record if needed, save all takes, and name files exactly as specified in the project folder.

Activity 13.3 – Suno AI Soundtrack Workflow: Students copy their existing NotebookLM L12 prompt directly into Suno AI without rewriting it, generate 2–3 versions, critically evaluate each against their game's tone and hook moment, refine by changing one small detail if needed, and export the best version as Soundtrack_Final.mp3.

Activity 13.4 – Trailer Voiceover with ElevenLabs: Students paste their 1–2 voiceover lines from their L12 plan into ElevenLabs Text to Speech, select a voice matching their game's tone, adjust speed/stability/clarity sliders if needed, generate 2 versions, evaluate critically for naturalness and audience fit, and export the strongest version as Voiceover_Final.mp3.

Stretch Task – AI Trailer Visual Enhancement: Students use Meshy AI to create a cinematic version of an existing character, boss, prop, or iconic object from their game. They sketch or screenshot the original, upload to Meshy for a refined 3D version, compare both side by side, and write a one-sentence reflection on authenticity. Evidence required: original screenshot, Meshy version, and reflection.

Reflection – Human Creativity Check: Students write 3 specific examples of elements in their game that are uniquely human — mechanics inspired by personal experience, dialogue reflecting personality, design choices from personal taste, problems solved creatively, or emotions intended for players — explaining why AI could not generate each without knowing them.

Extension Task – Alternative Asset Exploration: Students return to Suno AI and generate an alternative soundtrack with a deliberately different genre, tempo, or mood (using a modified prompt), then return to ElevenLabs and apply a different voice style to the same voiceover lines. Students compare both pairs of assets and write a one-sentence justification for their primary selection based on target audience fit.

Draw the connection between over-engineering and loss of clarity. Emphasize that constraints are a professional superpower, not a limitation. Spend 3–5 minutes here before moving to objectives.

Curriculum Alignment – Lesson 12: Scope Freeze & Finalisation. Implementing a structured scope freeze and making final DONE / FIX / CUT decisions aligns with UK D&T KS3 1d (evaluating outcomes and improving designs through iteration and informed decision-making), AQA 3.1.1 (evaluating and refining digital products using user feedback and performance data), IB Design Criterion C & D (creating solutions and evaluating their success against explicit criteria), and CSTA 3A-AP-17 (systematically testing, refining, and finalising computational artifacts).

Explain scope freeze as a formal, intentional decision — not just "stopping." Use the analogy of a restaurant menu: chefs don't add new dishes on the night of a dinner service. Ask: "When does building become a way of avoiding launching?" Pause for responses. This concept may feel counterintuitive to students who love adding features — acknowledge that tension directly.

This is a pivotal conceptual moment — slow down here. Read the two options aloud and let them sit. Ask: "Be honest — which category are most of your decisions in?" Do not shame students who over-relied on AI, but make clear that professional accountability requires owning your choices. This connects to the OECD AI literacy framework. Write "AI informs. Humans decide." on the board.

Give students exactly 3 minutes to count and record their ME/AI/BOTH totals. Then ask for a show of hands: "Who had more than half as AI?" Do not single students out. Use aggregate data to frame a brief class discussion. Connect to K3.3 and K4.1 — explain these are real frameworks used by OECD to evaluate AI literacy in professional settings.
