metadata.title: Video Trailer Creation
core_topics: Pacing and structure in video editing
ai_focus: AI-generated video using Runway ML
Trade-offs between AI automation and manual creative control
Creative ownership vs. automation (Automation ≠ Ownership)
AI content attribution across multiple tools (Suno AI, ElevenLabs, Gemini, ChatGPT, NotebookLM, CapCut AI)
Generative AI pattern-based output vs. real game knowledge
Training data bias in AI voice generation tools
Misinformation risk from AI-generated trailers misrepresenting gameplay
Human oversight and accountability for AI-assisted creative work
AI literacy — evaluating AI output rather than accepting it wholesale
learning_objectives: Evaluate the trade-offs between AI-generated video and manual creative control.
Construct a 15–20 second structured game trailer using CapCut and pre-planned assets.
metadata.activity_type: mixed — individual reflection, pair work, whole-class discussion, and individual trailer creation
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with a Starter: Watch & Discuss activity (Slide 6). Students spend 2 minutes individually reacting to the question 'Would you trust AI to generate your entire trailer?' and writing a response. Then in pairs (3 minutes), they complete an evidence builder table comparing AI-Generated Trailers (Runway ML) vs. Manually Edited Trailers (CapCut), listing advantages and risks for each approach.

Slide 7 (Explore: AI-Generated Video vs. Manual Editing) presents a teacher-led debrief on the key insight that 'Automation ≠ Ownership': AI offers fast production and automated visual generation but risks generic, template output and limited creative ownership, while CapCut manual editing provides full creative control, authentic gameplay footage, and stronger brand identity.

Slide 8 (Teacher Demo – CapCut): The teacher demonstrates CapCut's interface — uploading gameplay clips, dragging onto the timeline, trimming and splitting clips for pacing, aligning voiceover, previewing, and exporting. Focus is on pacing and clarity, not effects.

Activity 14.1: Build Your Trailer (Slides 9–10, 25 minutes): Students build their game trailer in CapCut following their Lesson 12 structure exactly. The trailer must follow three sections: Hook (2–3 seconds, create immediate curiosity or tension), Gameplay (8–12 seconds, show core mechanic clearly), and Game Details (3–5 seconds, brand name, tagline, Endstar deeplink via Social Media Poster). Students import gameplay clips, Suno soundtrack, ElevenLabs voiceover, and social media poster/end card image. Detailed steps include arranging clips to match structure, syncing audio to each section, adding the social poster as an end card, checking text readability and audio balance, and exporting at 1080p. Editing tips emphasise splitting clips, cutting menus and pauses, speeding up slow moments (1.2x–1.5x), using quick transitions, lowering music under voiceover, and keeping the trailer to 15–20 seconds maximum. A reference video with clear CapCut steps is provided.

Slide 11 displays an Example Gameplay Trailer for student reference.

Slide 12 (AI Content Attribution Checklist): Students complete a structured table documenting which AI tools they used (Suno AI, ElevenLabs, Gemini, ChatGPT, NotebookLM, CapCut AI), what each tool generated, and how they modified or curated the output. Students then write a personal attribution statement explaining their AI use and creative decisions.

Slide 13 (Reflection: Trailer Creation): Students take 3 minutes to reflect individually on whether their trailer clearly communicates their game's core idea, whether the pacing is engaging from the first 3 seconds, one improvement they would make next time, and an AI-specific reflection on where AI supported their workflow and where they made the final decisions.

Slide 14 (Homework): Students must finish editing their trailer (15–20 seconds), tighten pacing, ensure audio alignment (voiceover + soundtrack), check that visuals clearly represent gameplay, and export the final version before next lesson.

Slide 15 (Extension: Cinematic Enhancement Challenge): Advanced students use Runway ML or CapCut AI to add 1–2 subtle cinematic improvements (lighting, transitions, pacing), save both original and AI-enhanced versions, watch them side-by-side, and write a 3-sentence reflection on whether AI improved quality or reduced authenticity. Students must ensure AI enhancements do not misrepresent actual gameplay.

Slide 16 (Self-Assessment): Students reflect on whether they met All, Most, or Some of the success criteria and record one improvement target.

Slides 17–19 (Learning Check): Three MCQ practice questions on AI literacy topics — AI pattern-based generation vs. real game knowledge (MCQ 14.1), training data and bias in AI voice tools (MCQ 14.2), and the greatest risk of fully automating trailer creation without human review (MCQ 14.3). Completed in Classkick.
